captions.text: Aphelion is the new one by Don't Nod, the  French developer I've been checking in with
now and again over the years, who also brought  us Banishers: Ghosts of New Eden, Vampyr,
and Remember Me and My Very Prominent Bottom.  I'm broadly fond of them as a dev because they're
creative in spirit and rarely go back to the  same genre twice, in either story or gameplay,
although it must be said they've apparently  hit upon a character dynamic they like,
because the two leads in Aphelion are  basically the two leads from Banishers again:
smarty-pants black lady leader with slightly  hard-to-place accent; hairy, white, British,
not-quite-as-smart sidekick man. Also with  slightly hard-to-place accent. The game says
he's British, but by the sound of it, he must've  grown up in the fuckin' French Quarter of Milton
Keynes or something. Anyway, Aphelion is Don't  Nod having a crack at good old-fashioned hard
sci-fi about astronauts from a dying Earth  coming to an alien world to find a new home
for humanity and see if they can beat their  "fucking over planets" speed record. I suspect
Don't Nod had trouble coming up with the title.  "What shall we call it?" "Oh, I don't know." "I
don't know either. Wanna break for lunch?" "Yeah,  ah feel ya on that one." "WAIT A FUCKING MINUTE."
Continuing the Banishers comparison, Aphelion  also opens with the two leads, Ariane and Thomas,
in bed together, although in this case  with an air of regret and awkwardness,
probably because neither of them realized they  were supposed to take their underpants off.
Their spaceship then crashes on the frozen planet  they were heading for 'cause maybe they should've
been watching the rearview mirror instead  of having a disappointing shag, and Ariane
wakes up alone in the wreckage and sets out to  explore the planet to find Thomas. Mysteriously,
Thomas is never seen during the crash sequence,  so I really thought there was going to be a Silent
Hill 2-esque twist where it turned out Ariane  accidentally killed Thomas earlier with exploding
blue balls and was blotting out the memory,  but that got refuted pretty quick the first
time control switches to Thomas on another  part of the planet. Gameplay goes back and
forth between the two chapter by chapter as they  attempt to reunite, survive the hostile landscape,
uncover some mysteries, complete their mission  to save humanity, and maybe irrigate the Sahara
and solve Fermat's Last Theorem while  we're piling up the fucking workload.
As for what gameplay Don't Nod are experimenting  with this time, Aphelion will happily throw out
the ever-unhelpful "action-adventure" descriptor,  and the tone is set right out of the gate when the
first thing Ariane does is open a door to reveal  a steep downhill slope that she immediately
slides down on her bum, dodging the obstacles  that appear with remarkably good timing. "Oh,
I see," I said to myself. "It's not just a  third-person action-adventure game. It's THE
third-person action-adventure game." The standard  model, more or less. Hope you like following a
perfectly linear path of yellow climbing ledges  and getting immediately murdered if you attempt
to go anywhere else. Sliding down slopes on  her arse comes up quite a lot in Ariane's path,
which is one thing if there's a floor collapse and  she's forced into bum sliding against her will,
quite another when she's happily leaping  straight into a bum slide before even checking
for alternatives; I'd have thought she'd  be mindful of the essential life-preserving
equipment strapped to her back that probably  hasn't been rated for fucking winter sports.
Thomas, meanwhile, has been injured and can't  do any climbing because he has to at all times
keep at least one hand pressed against his  hurty tummy. So his gameplay is more about
slowly walking around places, solving the  occasional extremely pedestrian puzzle.
For example, there's a locked door with a  six-digit code lock, and in the room right next
to it, there's a very conspicuous birthday card  on a corkboard. Yeah, that old fuckin' chestnut.
The digital age's equivalent of pencil, keyhole,  piece of paper. So we can add another task to
these dudes' workload alongside saving humanity  and finding each other: finding a fucking core
gameplay mechanic to stick with. A small amount of  skill challenge is added to the climbing by having
you press a button to grab a ledge, but if you  don't, you still grab the ledge, you just have
to press a different button within a timer to  avoid losing your grip, which as game mechanics
go is about as organic and stimulating as a  fucking popup window with two buttons reading,
"Would you like to die now, yes or no?" Besides  that, you might have noticed that the Steam tags
insist on calling Aphelion a stealth game.  Perhaps with a shrug and a mumble of "Well,
we gotta call it something." There are indeed bits  where we get hunted by a big blind floating alien
snake thing that's a sort of manifested middle  finger of the planet itself trying to repel the
invaders, which is about as on the nose as a plot  element in a game with environmental themes gets.
So every now and again, we must indulge that  ever-welcome trope, the forced stealth section,
against a wibbly-wobbly visual effect  of unclear physicality, often through
confusing cave environments where I'm not even  sure where I'm supposed to be stealthing to and
the wibbly-wobbly death snake doesn't seem to  have any clearer notion. So excuse me while I
decide upon what asshole dismissive simile  to deploy here. How do we feel about "like
navigating a pitch-black bedroom, trying to avoid  waking a sleeping cat that's lying on top of a
Roomba"? And perhaps I'll follow that up with  a classic subverted simile. "It's about as fun
as a very not-fun thing." There's something  very iffy about Aphelion—iffelion?—in a way
that makes it hard for me to overlook little  annoyances. I hate how the game forces us to
wait for every animation and dialogue line to  finish before we can interact with anything else,
so I'm in a lab staring at a very obvious computer  with a Post-it note on the monitor saying,
"the answers to all of your questions,"  waiting to press the fucking contextual
button prompt that hasn't lit up yet because  my character hasn't finished pontificating
on how astonishing it is to have found  a Post-it note thriving in the wild.
Also, did you invest a lot of money in facial  animation recently, Don't Nod? 'Cause you
suddenly seem weirdly obsessed with sticking the  camera right in characters' faces as they react
with surprise or consternation at something and  their eyes and mouth muscles elaborately twitch
like stroke victims trying to walk across  Lego. In the rankings of AA developers,
I would classify Don't Nod as more of  an Average Alan than an Amazing Andrew,
but I also wouldn't lump them in with the  Anus Adrians, and that's what makes Aphelion
such a Disappointing Daniel. It's getting some  very stern notes at the bottom of its homework
written in very emphatic red Biro. "Not your best  work. Must try harder. See me." In a nutshell,
it's 10 hours of utterly bog-standard ghost train  ride gameplay in which the main characters fail
their way across a few pretty environments,  achieving very little, chasing a couple of
intrigues and plot threads that ultimately don't  really go anywhere. But having said all that,
there's an agreeable message at the heart of  this narrative. Let's be kind to the planet,
it says. Let's be responsible stewards of our  corner of the universe. 'Cause if we don't,
we might have to go look for new planets and end  up having to play through a slightly boring video
game. Message received. Now I just need  to figure out which bin to sort it into.
